title: Why your body temperature fluctuates with emotional activation
author: Kalesh

Your body isn't broken. It's tracking threat.

htmlContent:
Your body isn't broken. It's tracking threat.

I remember sitting in a coffee shop last winter. The room was warm, radiator hissing, and yet my hands were ice cold. My friend asked if I was okay. I said I was fine. But my body was telling a different story. I'd just finished a conversation that stirred up old grief, and my temperature was dropping like I'd stepped into a freezer.

Here's the thing. Your body temperature fluctuates with emotional activation. Not because something is wrong with you. But because your nervous system is doing exactly what it evolved to do. It's scanning for safety and threat, and it uses temperature as one of its tools. Stay with me here.

When you feel suddenly hot during a panic attack, or cold when you're dissociating, or clammy when you're anxious, that's not random. That's your autonomic nervous system shifting gears. And understanding why can change how you relate to your body. It can reduce the shame. It can help you stop thinking you're broken.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, spent decades mapping how our nervous system responds to cues of safety and danger. He found that the vagus nerve, a major highway between brain and body, regulates heart rate, digestion, and yes, body temperature. When you perceive threat, your ventral vagal system (the social engagement branch) downshifts. Your sympathetic nervous system revs up. Blood moves away from your skin and toward your large muscles. You get cold hands. You get pale. You're preparing to fight or flee.

And when the threat is overwhelming? When escape feels impossible? Your dorsal vagal system kicks in. This is the oldest, most primitive branch. It drops your heart rate, lowers your blood pressure, and can make you feel cold, numb, or heavy. This is the freeze response. And it's not a choice. It's a survival strategy.

Think about it. If you've ever felt a chill run down your spine during a scary memory, or gotten hot flashes during a flashback, you've experienced this. Your body is not betraying you. It's trying to protect you.

Why you get hot when you're angry and cold when you're scared

Anger and fear are both sympathetic states, but they feel different in the body. Anger often comes with heat. Your face flushes. Your chest feels tight and warm. This is because anger mobilizes you for action. Blood rushes to your extremities. Your muscles prepare to push, to strike, to defend. It's a forward-moving energy.

Fear, on the other hand, often brings coldness. Your hands get icy. Your feet feel like blocks of ice. This is because fear also mobilizes you, but with a different quality. Blood goes to your core, to your heart and lungs, to prepare for running. Your peripheral vessels constrict. You lose heat from your surface. It's a protective cooling.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how trauma gets stored in the body. In his book Waking the Tiger (paid link), he describes how animals in the wild shake off the effects of threat once the danger passes. They don't hold onto the activation. But humans do. We freeze. We suppress. And that frozen activation can show up as chronic temperature dysregulation.

I've had clients tell me they feel hot all the time, or cold all the time, and they don't know why. Often, it's because their nervous system is stuck in a particular state. Chronic hyperarousal (sympathetic dominance) can make you feel perpetually warm or sweaty. Chronic hypoarousal (dorsal vagal dominance) can make you feel cold and distant from your own body.

This is not about your thermostat being broken. It's about your nervous system being locked in a survival pattern. And the good news is, those patterns can shift.

The role of the vagus nerve in temperature regulation

The vagus nerve is a long, wandering nerve that connects your brainstem to your heart, lungs, stomach, and intestines. It's a key player in the parasympathetic nervous system, the rest-and-digest branch. But it's not a simple on-off switch. The vagus nerve has multiple branches, each with different functions.

The myelinated ventral vagus, the newer branch, is responsible for social engagement and calm connection. When it's active, your heart rate is steady, your breathing is easy, and your body temperature feels balanced. You feel warm and present. You can connect with others.

The unmyelinated dorsal vagus, the older branch, is responsible for the freeze response. When it's activated, your heart rate drops, your blood pressure falls, and your body temperature can drop too. You might feel cold, numb, or disconnected. This is the branch that gets triggered in trauma survivors, especially those who experienced helplessness or inescapable threat.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), has shown how trauma survivors often have difficulty regulating their body temperature. In his research, he found that people with PTSD showed altered autonomic responses to stress. Their bodies didn't return to baseline easily. They stayed hot or cold longer than people without trauma histories.

This isn't a character flaw. It's a nervous system adaptation. Your body learned to respond to threat in a specific way, and that response includes temperature shifts. The goal isn't to eliminate those shifts. It's to build capacity to regulate them.

How to work with temperature shifts instead of fighting them

So what do you do when you feel your body temperature changing in response to emotional activation? The first step is to notice without judgment. I know that sounds simple, but it's actually quite hard. Most of us have been taught to ignore our bodies, to push through discomfort, to pretend we're fine. But that only keeps the nervous system stuck.

Instead, try this. When you notice a temperature shift, pause. Put a hand on your chest or belly. Take a slow breath. Notice what you're feeling without trying to change it. You might say to yourself, "I'm feeling cold right now. That's okay. My body is doing what it needs to do."

You can also use physical tools to support regulation. A warm blanket can help when you feel cold. A cool cloth on your wrists can help when you feel hot. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link), which can reduce sensory overwhelm and help your nervous system settle. When your environment feels less threatening, your body temperature often follows.

Another approach is to orient to safety. Look around the room. Find something that feels neutral or pleasant. A lamp. A plant. The color of the wall. Notice it fully. This activates the ventral vagal system and can help your body warm up or cool down naturally.

And if you're working with a therapist or on your own, consider exploring somatic approaches. A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) by Deb Dana. It offers exercises for tracking and shifting your nervous system states, including temperature changes.

What your temperature shifts are telling you

Your body temperature is not just a random fluctuation. It's a signal. It's telling you something about your internal state. Maybe you're feeling threatened. Maybe you're feeling disconnected. Maybe you're feeling a wave of emotion that you haven't fully processed.

I've learned to pay attention to my own temperature shifts. When I feel cold during a conversation, I know I'm either scared or dissociating. When I feel hot, I know I'm angry or anxious. And instead of judging myself for it, I try to respond with curiosity. What just happened? What triggered this shift? What does my body need right now?

This is not about controlling your temperature. It's about listening to it. It's about understanding that your body is always communicating, and that communication includes temperature. Your nervous system is not your enemy. It's your ally, trying to keep you safe.

Wild, right? The same system that makes you cold when you're scared is the same system that makes you warm when you're safe. It's all connected. And the more you understand that connection, the less scary these shifts become.

Sit with that for a moment. Your body temperature fluctuations are not a sign of weakness. They're a sign that your nervous system is alive and working. And that's something to be grateful for, even when it's uncomfortable.
